title: Address to the 77th Session of the UN General Assembly
titleOriginal: Виступ на 77-й сесії Генеральної Асамблеї ООН
occasion: 77th Session of the UN General Assembly
sourceLabel: Office of the President of Ukraine

paragraphs.text: Mr. President of the General Assembly, Secretary-General, heads of state and government, ladies and gentlemen. I am speaking to you on behalf of a state that has been forced to defend its very existence, its territory, its people, its values. A state under massive, unprovoked, and unjustifiable invasion by the Russian Federation.

This speech was supposed to be delivered in person. However, I want you to understand what is happening in my country. I want you to feel it. That is why I asked the General Assembly to allow this address by video. Because a war is being waged not just against Ukraine, but against the principles upon which this very organization was built.

A crime has been committed against Ukraine, and we demand just punishment. The crime was committed against our state borders. The crime was committed against the lives of our people. The crime was committed against the dignity of our women and men. Against our children. Against every Ukrainian family. A crime against the truth itself.

Russia has waged war against our energy infrastructure. When you hear the word 'winter,' you think of holidays, family, hot cocoa, warm fireplaces. I will tell you what the word 'winter' means for millions of Ukrainians. It means the darkness that descends when Russia destroys our power plants. It means parents wondering how they will keep their children warm.

Ukraine wants peace. Europe wants peace. The world wants peace. And we have seen who is the only one who wants war. There is only one entity among all the U.N. member states that would say now, if it could, that it is satisfied with this war, with this invasion. Russia, and only Russia.

We can return the Ukrainian flag to our entire territory. We can do it by force of arms. But we need time. We need you to be more resolute. And I know that you can be. I see this General Assembly and I know that every nation here values sovereignty, values the sanctity of borders, because every nation here has borders that must be respected.

Russia is afraid of real negotiations. Russia is afraid of genuine peace. That is why it resorts to nuclear blackmail. You have all heard the threats. Russia threatens the world with nuclear weapons. This is not a bluff, they say. But let me tell you what is a bluff: the belief that the world will simply stand by and allow nuclear terrorism.

A nuclear state is threatening other states with nuclear weapons. That alone should compel every member of this body to act. If you think this is only about Ukraine, you are wrong. Today it is Ukraine. Tomorrow it will be someone else. Your country, your sovereignty, your borders. Because if tyranny is allowed to succeed here, it will be emboldened everywhere.

Let me remind you of what Russia has done. It seized Crimea in 2014. It occupied parts of the Donbas. The world imposed some sanctions and hoped it would be enough. It was not enough. Appeasement only feeds aggression. And in February of this year, Russia launched a full-scale invasion of a sovereign state, a founding member of the United Nations.

I want to share with you five conditions for peace. These are not just Ukraine's conditions. They are the conditions of the United Nations Charter. Sovereignty must be restored. Territorial integrity must be upheld. Those who committed crimes must be held accountable. The damage inflicted must be compensated. And there must be guarantees that this will never happen again.

We have witnessed mass graves in Izium, just as we witnessed them in Bucha. Torture chambers where civilians were subjected to unspeakable cruelty. Filtration camps where Ukrainians were detained and screened. Forced deportation of our children to Russian territory. These are not the methods of the twenty-first century. These are the methods of those who wish to destroy a nation.

Ukraine has demonstrated that we can fight back. We liberated territory that many believed was lost forever. Kharkiv Oblast was freed by the courage of our soldiers. But military success alone is not enough. We need the solidarity of nations. We need the world to remain united, because unity is the only answer to aggression.

I want to thank every nation that has supported Ukraine. Every nation that imposed sanctions against Russia. Every nation that provided weapons, humanitarian aid, shelter for our refugees. You are not just helping Ukraine. You are protecting the international order. You are safeguarding the rule of law. And I assure you, history will remember those who stood on the right side.

We must also address the global consequences of this war. The food crisis caused by Russia's blockade of our ports. Millions of people in Africa and Asia depend on Ukrainian grain. Russia has weaponized hunger. It has turned food into a tool of geopolitical coercion. The Black Sea Grain Initiative must be defended and expanded.

Let me say this very clearly: Ukraine will not give up any part of its territory. We will not trade our people's freedom for a false peace. We will not agree to frozen conflicts designed to give the aggressor time to rearm. Peace must be just. Peace must be comprehensive. And peace must be based on the full restoration of Ukraine's sovereignty.

To those who call for dialogue and negotiation, I say: we are ready. Ukraine has always been ready. But there can be no dialogue when one side holds a gun to your head and demands you surrender. There can be no negotiation when an aggressor is occupying your land and killing your people. First, the aggression must stop. Then, we can talk.

I address this General Assembly with a simple request. Do not look away. Do not grow tired of this war. Do not allow yourselves to become numb to the suffering of the Ukrainian people. Because every day that this war continues, lives are lost. Every day, families are destroyed. Every day, the principles of this organization are tested.

Let this Assembly be remembered as the one that stood up. That answered the call. That proved the United Nations is not just a building, not just a set of speeches, but a force for justice in the world. Ukraine is fighting for its survival. But Ukraine is also fighting for you. For the values that bind us. For the Charter that defines us. For the peace that unites us. Thank you. Glory to Ukraine.
